sound_type: overlapping bark cacophony -- multiple California Sea Lions barking simultaneously at urban waterfront haul-out site; docks, piers, buoys in Puget Sound and Elliott Bay; 200-8,000 Hz; the most familiar and culturally recognized Pacific Northwest marine mammal vocalization; E=7
ecosystem: Urban waterfront -- docks, piers, floating platforms, navigation buoys, marina infrastructure in Puget Sound, Elliott Bay, and Salish Sea
season: Winter (non-breeding; October-April; the largest winter aggregations in Pacific Northwest)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Eastern stock ~300,000-350,000; substantially increased since MMPA protection (1972). Recovery success story. Current conflicts: ESA-listed fish predation (Ballard Locks steelhead case), dock infrastructure use, vessel interactions.

coordinates.description: Urban waterfront haul-out sites: Elliott Bay Marina, Shilshole Bay, Port of Seattle waterfront, Ballard Locks area, various Puget Sound piers and buoys. Winter resident.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Bark -- short, percussive, the classic 'arf arf.' Different from Steller Sea Lion roar (sustained, lower frequency). Produced rapidly in sequences.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 200-8,000 Hz; energy concentrated 500-3,000 Hz. Well within human hearing range. Higher-pitched and more percussive than Steller (100-1,000 Hz).
acoustic_data.chorus_character: Multiple simultaneous barks at haul-out; different animals at different rates and pitches (males lower, females and juveniles higher); overlapping continuous sound. Audible at considerable distance from active haul-out sites in Elliott Bay.
acoustic_data.male_additional: Adult males also produce a longer, lower-pitched bark and a trumpet vocalization for territorial display. Full male repertoire: bark, trumpet, threat vocalizations.
acoustic_data.mother_pup: Individual vocal recognition system: stereotyped pup-attraction call + pup response. Operates within dock chorus acoustic environment.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7: constant overlapping bark cacophony; less than Steller bull roar (E=8: larger animal, sustained, lower frequency); more than Dall's Porpoise (E=6: primarily ultrasonic, human-inaudible). The familiar urban bark at sustained overlapping intensity.

nodes.title: Second and Final Otariidae -- Chapter Closes
nodes.description: Degree 284 is the second Otariidae entry following Steller Sea Lion (283). Otariidae chapter (degrees 283-284) documents both PNW eared seal species: Steller (large, remote, Endangered, summer, E=8) and California (medium, urban, Least Concern, winter, E=7). Arc complete.

nodes.title: Dock Chorus -- Urban Waterfront Haul-Out
nodes.description: California Sea Lions haul out on docks, piers, buoys, and marina infrastructure -- unlike Steller's preference for remote rocky sites. Elliott Bay, Shilshole Bay, Port of Seattle waterfront. Winter October-April. The bark chorus is audible at distance. E=7 urban cacophony.

nodes.title: Steller vs. California -- The Otariidae Contrasts
nodes.description: 283 vs. 284: E=8 vs. E=7; 2,500 lb vs. 900 lb; roar 100-1,000 Hz vs. bark 200-8,000 Hz; remote rookery vs. urban dock; summer vs. winter; Endangered vs. Least Concern; named for 18th-c. naturalist vs. named for California coast. Complete opposite profiles within the same family.

nodes.title: Ballard Locks Conflict -- ESA vs. MMPA
nodes.description: California Sea Lions learned to prey on ESA-listed winter-run steelhead at Hiram M. Chittenden Locks fish ladder. Recovering protected species preying on endangered species. MMPA eventually authorized lethal removal of individual predating animals. Defining Pacific Northwest marine mammal management case.

nodes.title: Marine Mammal Arc Closes -- Three Families, Four Degrees
nodes.description: 281-284 marine mammal arc: Dall's Porpoise (Phocoenidae, E=6) → Pacific White-sided Dolphin (Delphinidae, E=7) → Steller Sea Lion (Otariidae, E=8) → California Sea Lion (Otariidae, E=7). Three families, four degrees, energy arc 6→7→8→7. Complete.

cross_references.relationship: First Otariidae, E=8. The contrast pair: large/remote/Endangered/summer/roar vs. medium/urban/LC/winter/bark. Same family; opposite ecological and acoustic profiles. Chapter comparison.
Marine mammal arc beginning (E=6). Phocoenidae. The 281-284 arc: Phocoenidae → Delphinidae → Otariidae → Otariidae. Three families, ascending then returning. Arc complete at 284.

E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Block closes. Self-released garage punk paired with urban dock bark chorus. Both unceremonious, both in available space, both doing their thing at E=7 without grandeur. The block's final pairing.
